headlines.title: Firefighters battle 79-acre wildfire in northern Napa County
headlines.description: Firefighters battled nearly an 80-acre wildfire Monday night in a rural area of northern Napa County, successfully preventing any structural damage. The Hardin Fire, which ignited around 2:40 p.m. near Hardin and Pope Canyon roads, was 60% contained by 9 p.m. Monday. The blaze burned primarily in annual grasses with a moderate rate of spread. Improved mapping later updated the fire’s size to 79 acres, according to Cal Fire. “Crews are making good progress on it,” said Jason Clay, a spokesperson for the Cal Fire Sonoma-Lake-Napa Unit. “There are no evacuation orders or warnings with that fire.” A rapid, coordinated response from the air and ground kept the agricultural area’s properties safe. Napa County Fire Department volunteer firefighters played a critical role in the effort, deploying three water tenders and one engine to the incident. Volunteers also staffed an additional engine to ensure continued fire protection for the nearby Spanish Flat community and the Lake Berryessa area. With temperatures expected to rise throughout the week, fire officials are urging the public to prevent wildfires by avoiding activities that could spark a blaze, properly securing trailer chains, and reporting any smoke immediately by calling 911. Bay City News contributed to this report.

headlines.title: Longtime USF downtown cafe faces closure after nearly 35 years
headlines.description: For nearly 35 years, Sweet Joanna’s Cafe has been a neighborhood staple at the University of San Francisco’s downtown campus, serving students, staff and local workers. But after surviving the severe economic downturns of recent years, the cafe’s owner says he was shocked to learn last month that the university does not plan to renew his lease. Operating on the first floor of the historic Folgers Coffee building at 101 Howard St., just a block from the Embarcadero, Sweet Joanna’s has weathered the neighborhood’s ups and downs. “In the pandemic we went through the worst. And I thought, okay, the good time is coming,” owner Joseph Abughosh said. “I don’t know about the good time anymore.” The sudden notice prompted Abughosh to rally his customers. Alongside a “Best of 2026” award and a certificate of honor from the city, a blunt poster recently hung on the cafe wall reading: After 35 years, USF is throwing us out. Over the past few weeks, Abughosh has encouraged patrons to email the university to voice their concerns. Customers have echoed his frustration, pointing to the cafe’s reliable service and affordable pricing in an increasingly expensive area. “I’m surprised,” customer Iain Clayton said. “I mean, it’s been a great place to come, the food’s good and it’s pretty cheap.” For USF graduate student Michael Vostrikov, the cafe is a vital resource. “Considering it’s on a college campus, here you can get something to eat [for] 15 bucks after tax,” Vostrikov said. “Anywhere else it’s 20-plus. And you’re in a situation like, you don’t really make a lot going to college.” Abughosh added that USF staff are among his most loyal patrons, often calling him at late hours for last-minute catering help, which he says he has always been happy to provide. In a prepared statement, USF spokesperson Mary McInerney said the university plans to transition to a new food service partner but has not yet selected one. “The university’s decision was made only after careful consideration,” McInerney wrote. “It was not an easy decision, nor does it diminish our appreciation for the cafe’s long history.” Despite the impending closure, Abughosh recently expressed renewed optimism after receiving a personal phone call from the university president. “He was optimistic, actually he was kind and nice, and I know he’s a man of God,” Abughosh said. “So I feel that, maybe he wants to work something out with me.” As a show of good faith, Abughosh took down most of the protest posters inside the business. While he has not received further contact from the university and his future at 101 Howard St. remains uncertain, he and his staff continue to serve their daily customers.

headlines.title: Police fatally shoot ‘armed and dangerous' suspect in Oakland
headlines.description: An “armed and dangerous” suspect was fatally shot by Oakland police after he lunged at officers with a knife, officials said. The police shooting was reported 2 p.m. Monday at a shopping center parking lot in the area of 40th Avenue and International Boulevard. “Officers attempted to deescalate the situation by giving commands. The suspect refused,” Beere said at a news briefing following the shooting. “At which point the subject attacked the officers, causing the officers to use less lethal and lethal force.” The suspect succumbed to their injuries at the scene and the two officers involved in the shooting were not injured, Beere said. The police chief said the response stemmed from a California Highway Patrol investigation. CHP told NBC Bay Area the investigation involved a homeless encampment contact armed with a knife. When the suspect who had “dangerous weapons” ran away from CHP officers, the agency requested assistance from the Oakland Police Department. Police located the armed suspect at the shopping center parking lot prior to the deadly shooting. An investigation is ongoing. Oakland Jul 9 Oakland Jul 12 Oakland Jul 7

headlines.title: Bay Area drivers rush to gas pumps as U.S.-Iran conflict sends oil prices soaring
headlines.description: Escalating tensions between the U.S. and Iran are sending Bay Area drivers scrambling to the pumps as global oil prices surge. The rush follows an announcement by President Donald Trump that the U.S. will reimpose a blockade of Iranian ports near the Strait of Hormuz. Amid an exchange of military strikes between the two nations, Trump declared America “the guardian” of the strait and said the U.S. will impose a 20% fee on all shipped cargo. Following the announcement, U.S. crude oil prices soared 9.4%, closing at their highest level since mid-June. Energy experts warn the geopolitical conflict will soon hit consumers’ wallets. “If crude oil prices stay where they are, then gas will likely go up 5 to 10 cents next week,” said Severin Borenstein, a professor at the Energy Institute at the UC Berkeley Haas School of Business. However, Borenstein cautioned that the volatile situation makes long-term forecasting difficult. “There is so much uncertainty,” he said. “If there’s a peace deal, then prices may drop. If the U.S. starts bombing, prices will go up further.” Currently, the average cost of a gallon of regular gas in California is $5.38, according to AAA. While that is lower than last month’s average, it remains nearly 90 cents higher than a year ago. At Moe’s Gas Station in San Jose, lines of cars grew steadily as drivers kept a close eye on the Middle East, hoping to avoid anticipated price hikes. “I’m worried it’s going to go to six bucks, so I’m trying to fill up as early and as often as I can to try to lock in the lower prices while we have them,” said Dave Ortega of San Jose. For many drivers, the creeping prices are already causing financial strain. “It’s ridiculous. We are paying one hundred dollars each time,” said San Jose resident Elyas Rahgozar. “Before it was fifty-five or sixty, now it’s one hundred. It’s very bad.” Others voiced resignation over the fact that local prices are tied to a conflict thousands of miles away. “There’s nothing we can do about it,” said Kevin Chen of San Jose. The anticipated pain at the pump is already altering local travel plans, with several Bay Area residents saying they plan to scale back road trips originally scheduled for the end of summer.

headlines.title: Santa Clara County goes door-to-door to recover more than 1,000 Costco grapevines allegedly linked to invasive pest
headlines.description: Santa Clara County agricultural officials are taking extreme measures Monday—including going door-to-door—to track down more than 1,000 grapevine plants sold at Costco that may be harboring an invasive pest. The glassy-winged sharpshooter is a small insect capable of spreading Pierce’s disease, a condition that can decimate local vineyards, nurseries, and farms. Approximately 1,300 grapevine plants were shipped to the area from Fresno and Southern California and were subsequently purchased by local Costco shoppers in April and May. While Costco has successfully recovered about 200 of the plants so far, county officials are not waiting for the remaining customers to return them. Biologists began visiting homes on Monday to physically uproot and retrieve the plants, starting in Gilroy. “We really wanted to start here in South County because this is where a big part of our grape and wine industries [are] located, so we want to provide protection for our growers,” said Ericka Mora, Santa Clara County deputy agricultural commissioner. Following the collections in Gilroy, crews plan to expand their recovery efforts to San Jose. When crews visit a home, they remove the grapevine directly if the resident is present. If no one is home, workers leave a notice on the door equipped with bags, ties, and instructions on how to prepare the plant for a follow-up collection within two days. Vivian Volpe, a county associate biologist and standards specialist, emphasized the time-sensitive nature of the mission. “This is pretty urgent because we want to make sure that this is contained as soon as possible, just because of the fact that these plants have been in these homes for a little over a month,” Volpe said. Volpe noted that the physical removals have gone smoothly, as the recently planted vines do not yet have extensive root systems. She added that residents have been highly cooperative. “They wanted to make sure none of their plants got diseases either and to keep the community safe,” Volpe said. “It was a very good interaction all the way around.” County officials urge anyone who purchased the grapevine plants but has not yet been contacted to call the Santa Clara County agricultural department immediately. Experts warn that even if the glassy-winged sharpshooter is not visible on the plant, the insect may have already laid eggs, which poses an equal threat to local agriculture and crops.

headlines.title: 3 sailors rescued from San Francisco Bay after capsizing east of SFO
headlines.description: Three people were rescued from San Francisco Bay off San Mateo County on Saturday after their sailboat capsized. The trio was sailing near Coyote Point, which is south of San Francisco International Airport, when their boat tipped over at about 1:30 p.m. Deputies with the San Mateo County Sheriff’s Office Marine Unit who were training near the San Mateo-Hayward Bridge responded, the sheriff’s office said Monday. The boat capsized about a half mile east of the airport, the sheriff’s office said. The three sailors, who were Foster City residents, were cold from the water exposure but were otherwise uninjured. They were taken to Coyote Point Marina and evaluated by paramedics as a precaution. The boat was also towed to the marina by the sheriff’s office marine unit. Waters in that area of the Bay at that time were about 69 degrees, according to the U.S. Geological Survey.

headlines.title: Driver crashes into business in Novato
headlines.description: A driver crashed through a wall at a business in Novato Monday morning, according to police. Photos from the scene showed a sedan almost entirely inside HopMonk Tavern Novato, located at 224 Vintage Way. No injuries were reported. A cause for the crash wasn’t immediately known. HopMonk said the car crashed into the west wall of its music venue, forcing the business to temporarily close the space until the wall is fully repaired. HopMonk’s tavern and beer garden were not affected by the crash.

headlines.title: Ledson Fire: Forward progress stopped in Sonoma County blaze
headlines.description: A brush fire sparked an evacuation order Monday afternoon in Sonoma County, Cal Fire said. The so-called Ledson Fire in Santa Rosa burned about 13 acres along Sonomay Highway (Highway 12) and North Pythian Road. The area is near the Ledson Winery and Vineyards. The Sonoma County Sheriff’s Office during the response issued an evacuation order for Zone SON-3H3, which later was downgraded to a warning. Cal Fire at 3:17 p.m. reported stopping forward progress of the blaze. No injuries have been reported. The cause is under investigation. Bay City News contributed to this report. PG&E Jul 11 San Francisco Jul 9 bay area weather Jul 9 This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: Economists say ‘we must act now' on AI's economic impact and job displacement risks
headlines.description: Hundreds of economists say in an open letter that institutions “must act now” to address how artificial intelligence could transform the economy and could put many people out of work. The statement released Monday was signed by top economists, along with computer scientists and some executives at tech companies including Anthropic, Google and OpenAI. “AI may become radically more powerful over the next 10 years,” says the letter organized by Stanford University’s digital economy lab. “This could drive an unprecedented transformation of our economy, larger than the Industrial Revolution, but unfolding over a vastly shorter time frame. It could bring risks, including large-scale job displacement, as well as opportunities such as major gains in living standards.” The letter, which has only four sentences, says leaders must “build the incentives, guardrails, and institutions needed to steer AI in a direction that complements humans and benefits society.” The Stanford lab says the letter has so far been signed by more than 200 economists and AI researchers, including 16 winners of a Nobel Prize. Computer scientist and AI pioneer Yoshua Bengio was among the signatories and said in a separate statement that based on the trajectory of AI development, “it is highly plausible that AI will drastically transform our economies.” “We must be intentional and make collective, democratic choices, rather than letting market forces play out and risking leaving most citizens behind,” wrote Bengio, a professor at the University of Montreal.

headlines.title: Red Cross faces blood shortage, asks for donors
headlines.description: The American Red Cross has announced an emergency blood shortage Monday and is asking for donors to donate blood, platelets, or plasma as soon as possible. According to the organization, there has been a steep decline in blood donations since the end of May this year, creating a gap between supply and patient need. As a thank-you to donors, the organization is giving away a Fandango Movie Ticket of up to $15 in ticket price and fees via email for whoever donates from July 13-31. More details are available at RedCrossBlood.org/July.

headlines.title: Fairfield police continue to investigate deadly graduation shooting following arrest
headlines.description: Fairfield police recently made an arrest in last month’s deadly shooting following a graduation ceremony. A 17-year-old boy was arrested in Texas Tuesday for allegedly killing 18-year-old Jamario Baker shortly after his graduation from Sem Yeto High School in Fairfield last month, Fairfield police announced Friday. The suspect, who police say fled Fairfield for Texas within days of the shooting, was arrested on suspicion of murder and related offenses in the Dallas-Fort Worth area Tuesday morning with the help of the United States Marshals Service. He will remain in custody in Texas pending his extradition to Solano County. Around 7:15 p.m. on June 3, gunfire broke out in the parking lot of Sem Yeto High School, which is shared with Fairfield High School, following a graduation ceremony for seniors of Sem Yeto that afternoon. The gunman shot Baker and three others, ages 11, 20 and 25, who survived. “Through the course of the investigation, detectives identified the juvenile suspect and determined that he had fled California and traveled to the State of Texas within days of the shooting,” Fairfield police wrote in a statement. “Investigators conducted dozens of witness interviews, reviewed hours of surveillance and digital video, analyzed photographs and electronic records, executed search warrants, processed forensic evidence, and coordinated efforts across multiple jurisdictions to develop a comprehensive and prosecutable case,” according to Fairfield police. Police were scheduled to hold a 4 p.m. Monday news conference about the investigation, but canceled after reporting there was no further updates to share. The investigation remains active despite the arrest, said police. Anyone with information is urged to contact the Fairfield Police Department Investigations Division at 707-428-7600 or by email at juneincidenttips@fairfield.ca.gov.

headlines.title: Oakland moves forward with new deal for sale of Coliseum property
headlines.description: After years of failed negotiations and delayed plans, Oakland is moving forward with a new agreement to sell its portion of the Coliseum property for $125 million. Oakland city leaders said a revised deal to sell the city’s share of the Coliseum property to the Oakland Acquisition Company, which includes the African American Sports and Entertainment Group offering better financial terms and a path toward redeveloping the site. “It’s a deal that will bring in city revenue, create good paying jobs and drive real economic opportunities for our residents,” Mayor Barbara Lee said. Under the new agreement, the city would receive $50 million at closing, with the remaining payments made over time plus interest. The deal also gives the city a 6% annual share of future ticket fees. Oakland Jul 9 Alameda County May 28 Also included in the deal is a $5 million deposit that has already been made by the group. The new terms replaces a 2024 agreement. “We worked together to get the best deal we could do without breaking the deal,” Oakland councilman Ken Houston said. Iving Azoff, co-founder of the Oak View Group, sees the Coliseum as an opportunity to transform the property into a premier destination. “This is not a rescue mission in itself,” Azoff said. “It’s an opportunity to take what I consider a B experience and make it an A+ experience. We did it at the Forum. We’ve done it in numerous other places.” The new agreement follows years of controversy, negotiations and doubt about the future of the property. The deal still has its critics. Councilmember Noel Gallo cast the lone “no” vote, saying Oakland and Alameda County should present a united front on each of its 50% interest in the property. “The reality is they do a lot of talking, a lot of preaching, but at the end of the day it’s public land,” Gallo said. “It belongs to the public and Alameda County and Oakland need to be unified to represent the Oakland public to get this deal done.” The Oakland City Council is expected to take a final vote on the agreement later this month.
